behaviors.description: A file placed inside a subdirectory of the watched root is processed, not only files placed directly in the root directory.
behaviors.justification: Failure boundary is real filesystem watching behavior (recursive watch semantics and event delivery), not just in-process logic. MOCK-HIDE applies because mocked watcher callbacks can falsely pass even when OS-level recursive events are not wired correctly. Agentic/Workflow are disqualified by behavior text: this is filesystem monitoring only, no AI/ML decision steps.
behaviors.test_description: Start the real daemon process against a temp input/output directory tree. Create a nested subdirectory under the watched root, write a file into that subdirectory, and assert exactly one transformed output is produced for that nested file path within timeout.

behaviors.description: The deduplication logic correctly discards a second processing request for the same file path within a configurable time window.
behaviors.justification: Behavior text targets deduplication rule correctness for same path + time window, which can be validated at a single object/function boundary with injected clock/time values. No required OS/network/subprocess boundary is stated. Higher levels were considered and rejected: behavior text says "deduplication logic" (algorithmic rule), not watcher/OS event correctness.
behaviors.test_description: Instantiate the dedup component with a configurable window and deterministic clock. Submit request A for path P, then request B for the same P inside the window; assert B is dropped. Advance clock past the window and assert next request for P is accepted.

behaviors.description: When the operating system emits multiple filesystem events for a single file write, the daemon processes the file exactly once.
behaviors.justification: Behavior explicitly depends on OS emitting duplicate events. REAL-INFRA is satisfied directly; in-process fakes cannot reproduce true event burst patterns and timing races reliably. Agentic/Workflow are disqualified by behavior text: no model-based decisions, only file-event handling.
behaviors.test_description: Run the daemon with a real watched directory. Perform a write pattern known to trigger multiple events (e.g., temp-write+rename or chunked append). Observe event logs/output artifacts and assert only one processing run occurs for that file.

behaviors.description: If the watched directory is removed and then recreated while the daemon is running, the daemon resumes watching the new directory without a restart.
behaviors.justification: This is OS-level directory lifecycle/inode/watcher reattachment behavior. MOCK-HIDE applies because fake watchers usually do not model watch invalidation after remove/recreate. Higher levels rejected: no AI/ML output dependency and no multi-agent sequence in behavior text.
behaviors.test_description: Start daemon on a real temp watched path. Remove the watched directory, recreate it with the same path while daemon keeps running, then add a new file. Assert the file is processed without restarting the daemon.

behaviors.description: When multiple files arrive at the same time, they are processed in the order determined by their creation timestamps.
behaviors.justification: Ordering is determined from filesystem-derived creation timestamps under concurrent arrivals; correctness can fail due to real FS timestamp resolution/event timing, so System is the safest minimum. Integration was considered but rejected because behavior text ties order to actual "creation timestamps" at arrival time, which can differ from synthetic in-memory ordering.
behaviors.test_description: Run daemon against a real directory. Create multiple files in rapid succession (or controlled parallel writes) so arrivals overlap, capture processing order from daemon outputs/logs, and assert order matches each file’s filesystem creation timestamp as observed by the daemon.

behaviors.description: A file that is still being written to when the arrival event fires is not picked up for processing until writing is complete.
behaviors.justification: Failure depends on real write-in-progress timing and filesystem event behavior (partial writes, close/rename semantics). In-process mocks often hide this race by presenting only complete files. Agentic/Workflow are disqualified by behavior text: no AI/ML or multi-agent pipeline involved.
behaviors.test_description: Start daemon on a real watched directory. Write a large file in chunks with delays so an arrival event occurs before completion. Assert daemon does not process intermediate partial content and only processes after final write/close condition is met.
